messages.content:
### Summary: Introduction to Green Chemistry

Green chemistry represents more than a scientific field; it is a philosophy and a foundational approach to reshaping how science and engineering are conducted. The movement emphasizes sustainable practices through the following core areas:

**Core Elements and Methodologies:**
*   **Foundational Principles:** The discipline is built upon specific key elements that guide sustainable chemical practices (p. 1).
*   **Comprehensive Assessment:** Practitioners utilize Life-Cycle Analysis to evaluate the environmental impact of products and processes (p. 1).
*   **Risk Management:** A primary focus is placed on understanding and mitigating chemical hazards (p. 1).
*   **Technical Innovations:** The field explores sustainable alternatives, specifically:
    *   **Alternative Solvents:** Identifying safer, environmentally friendly substitutes for traditional solvents (p. 2).
    *   **Alternative Reagents:** Utilizing reagents that minimize waste and toxicity (p. 2).
*   **Design and Efficiency:** Improving the design of chemical reactions to enhance overall efficiency and performance (p. 2).

This approach, championed at professional forums such as the American Chemical Society Green Chemistry & Engineering Conferences, offers a path toward radicalizing traditional industrial and academic standards into a more sustainable model (p. 1).

Lesson 2: Green Chemistry Applications - Presentation6 slides
Green Chemistry Applications
  • Overview of diverse green chemistry applications.
  • Focus on sustainable synthesis and environmental remediation.

### Summary of Green Chemistry Applications

This document provides an overview of the book *Green Chemistry Applications*, edited by Murat Eyvaz and Ebubekir Yüksel. It explores the principles of green chemistry aimed at minimizing or eliminating hazardous materials throughout the lifecycle of chemical production, emphasizing preventative measures over remediation [S.13].

**Key Objectives and Content**
The publication addresses the necessity of aligning technological progress with environmental and health safety. It focuses on several critical areas:
*   **Industrial Innovation:** Researching alternative raw materials, non-volatile solvents, and non-hazardous catalysts to reduce waste at the source [S.13].
*   **Sustainability:** Promoting the use of rapidly renewable, reusable, and biodegradable materials alongside renewable energy sources [S.13].
*   **Technical Scope:** The text contains six chapters covering:
    *   Nanocarbon-polymer composites for energy and environmental uses [S.11].
    *   Synthesis of nanocomplexes via green chemistry techniques [S.11].
    *   Infrared irradiation as a green energy source [S.11].
    *   Click chemistry for green synthesis [S.11].
    *   Pharmaceutical pollution mitigation in water [S.11].
    *   Global water vapor estimation using sensor data [S.11].

**Editorial and Publishing Context**
*   **Editors:** Dr. Murat Eyvaz and Prof. Ebubekir Yüksel from the Environmental Engineering Department at Gebze Technical University, Turkey, bring extensive expertise in water treatment, electrochemical processes, and filtration to the work [S.9].
*   **Publisher:** Published in 2019 by IntechOpen, the book is part of an open-access initiative that prioritizes disseminating scientific knowledge globally [S.6, S.7].
*   **Legal Protections:** While the book as a whole is copyrighted by IntechOpen, individual chapters are distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 license, allowing for reproduction provided the original source and authors are credited [S.6].

This summary covers the provided text regarding recent developments and applications of nanocarbon-polymer composites.

### Overview and Motivation
The advancement of technology and industrialization has led to significant ecological pollution. There is an increasing research focus on sustainable nanocarbon-polymer composites to aid in environmental remediation and energy applications. Green chemistry principles are essential in this field to minimize toxicity during the synthesis of these "wonder materials" (p. 15-16).

### Carbon-Based Nanomaterial Classifications
Carbon’s unique valency allows for diverse allotropes with exceptional electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties at the nanoscale (1–100 nm). Key types include:

* Fullerenes (e.g., $C_{60}$): Spherical, hollow structures composed of pentagonal and hexagonal rings. They are valued as radical scavengers and for use in solar/fuel cells (p. 17).
* Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs): Cylindrical structures of graphene sheets. They are categorized as Single-Walled (SWCNT) or Multi-Walled (MWCNT). They offer high mechanical strength and electrical conductivity, making them ideal as fillers for composites (p. 17-18).
* Graphene: A two-dimensional, $sp^2$ hybridized single-atom layer arranged in a hexagonal lattice. It exhibits superior thermal and electrical conductivity compared to graphite and diamond (p. 18).
* Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs): Newly emerging nanoparticles ($<10$ nm) with unique photoluminescence properties. They are biocompatible and effective in sensing and photocatalysis (p. 17-18).

### Synthesis and Functionalization
While chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and laser ablation are common, there is a push toward environmentally friendly, inexpensive synthesis routes. Naturally occurring nanocarbons (e.g., found in volcanic lava or coal) exist but are difficult to control. Consequently, chemical functionalization—such as oxidation to attach carboxyl ($-COOH$) or hydroxyl ($-OH$) groups—is widely used to tailor the surface properties of nanocarbons for specific industrial requirements (p. 16, 18-19).

### Applications
The high aspect ratio, strength, and conductivity of nanocarbon-polymer composites enable their use in multiple sectors:

* Energy and Engineering: Lightweight, high-strength materials for turbines, automotive, and aviation parts to improve efficiency (p. 20).
* Electronics and Sensors: Used in flexible electronics, solar cells, and biochemical sensors due to excellent electrical conductivity and ductility (p. 20).
* Environmental: Employed in remediation and pollutant sensing, leveraging their active surface area and functional groups for efficient capture (p. 16-17).

This document covers the synthesis, characterization, and pharmacological evaluation of lanthanum(III) nanocomplexes produced via green chemistry methods.

### Key Points of Synthesis and Characterization
*   **Green Synthesis:** The study utilizes sonochemistry and organic ligands to synthesize La(III) complexes in nanoscale, adhering to green chemistry principles to reduce hazardous substances (p. 33, p. 35).
*   **Structural Analysis:** Physicochemical properties were confirmed using elemental analysis, IR spectroscopy, $^1$H NMR, UV-vis, TGA, and X-ray diffraction (XRD). These techniques verified the coordination of ligands to the La(III) ions and confirmed the monomeric, diamagnetic nature of the complexes (p. 36-39).
*   **Nanoscale Confirmation:** SEM and TEM imaging confirmed that the synthesized materials are nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 3.2 to 44.9 nm, often exhibiting spherical morphology (p. 39-41).
*   **Particle Size Determination:** The average particle size was calculated using the Debye-Scherrer equation:
    $L = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$ (p. 39).

### Biomedical Applications
*   **Anticancer Activity:** The nanocomplexes were tested for cytotoxicity against human epithelial colorectal adenocarcinoma (Caco2), Vero, and MCF-7 cell lines. The findings suggest the complexes are potent, selective anticancer agents capable of membrane penetration (p. 33, p. 40).
*   **Antimicrobial Impact:** The complexes successfully inhibited the growth of the *Klebsiella pneumonia* strain (p. 33).
*   **Wound Dressing:** An electrospinning technique was used to integrate these nanoparticles into chitosan-based nanofibers. The resulting bio-medicated nanofibers serve as a local chemotherapy delivery system for skin injuries (p. 33, p. 35).

This chapter explores the use of infrared (IR) irradiation as an efficient, sustainable, and "green" energy source for chemical synthesis and natural product extraction. Aligned with the UNESCO "Decade to Educate in the Sustainability" (2005–2014), the research demonstrates how IR irradiation—spanning near (NIR), middle (MIR), and far (FIR) zones—serves as an alternative to fossil-fuel-dependent conventional heating [S.49, S.50].

Key points regarding IR application include:

Organic Reaction Activation: IR irradiation promotes various transformations with high yields, shorter reaction times, and often under solvent-free conditions. Applications include Knoevenagel condensations, nucleophilic additions, multicomponent reactions (e.g., Biginelli, Hantzsch), redox procedures, and C-C bond formations such as the Mizoroki-Heck and Suzuki-Miyaura cross-couplings [S.51-S.56, S.58, S.59].

Natural Product Extraction: FIR and NIR have proven highly effective for extracting secondary metabolites from botanical sources such as habanero pepper, apricot, and rice. The use of NIR can preserve the integrity of cellular matrices better than traditional methods, resulting in cleaner and more efficient recovery of compounds like polyphenols and capsaicin [S.59, S.60].

Comparative Advantages: Research indicates that IR irradiation (or the combination of IR with ultrasound) frequently outperforms conventional mantle heating. Notable benefits include:
- Reduced energy consumption and elimination or minimization of solvents [S.49, S.50, S.60].
- Immediate response times and improved energy efficiency due to specific wavelengths, such as those provided by tungsten-halogen filaments or gold-plated reflectors [S.50, S.56].
- Enhanced physical properties of synthesized materials, such as increased surface area and smaller crystal size in photocatalysts like $SiO_2-TiO_2$ [S.60].

Conclusion: The authors conclude that as a versatile energy source, IR irradiation is a critical tool for green chemistry, with potential for further expansion into material engineering and complex synthetic strategies [S.61].

This research presents an efficient, green, and regioselective synthesis of 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazoles using click chemistry. The study evaluates the use of polyethylene glycol-400 (PEG-400) as an eco-friendly reaction medium (p. 71).

Key components and findings of the research include:

Methodology: The synthesis utilizes a multicomponent reaction involving substituted alkynes, organic halides, and sodium azide, catalyzed by copper iodide (CuI). This in situ generation of organic azides avoids the handling of hazardous or unstable azides (p. 71, 78).

Advantages: The use of PEG-400 offers significant synthetic benefits, including high atom economy, low environmental impact, mild reaction conditions (25–45°C), high yields, and short reaction times (10–45 min). Additionally, the solvent is commercially available, recyclable, and low in toxicity (p. 71, 72, 78).

Process: The reaction is carried out in 5 mL of PEG-400 with 10 mol% CuI. After completion, products are extracted with ethyl acetate and recrystallized from ethanol to achieve high purity (p. 72).

Applications: The resulting 1,2,3-triazoles are essential scaffolds in medicinal chemistry, known for activities such as anti-HIV and antimicrobial properties, and are useful in the production of agrochemicals and dyes (p. 71).

Chemical Formulas (Examples):
The general reaction forms derivatives such as $C_{15}H_{12}O_{2}N_{4}$ (p. 72) and $C_{16}H_{14}N_{4}O_{2}$ (p. 73). The copper-catalyzed cycloaddition reaction follows the general principle of Copper-catalyzed Azide-Alkyne Cycloaddition (CuAAC) (p. 78).

This document examines the advancement of green water treatment technologies, focusing on the removal of pharmaceutical pollutants and endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) through solar-driven photodecomposition processes (p. 67-68).

### Key Technologies and Environmental Impact
Treatment methods evaluated include natural solar irradiation, solar/Fe, solar/Fe/H2O2, and titanium oxide (TiO2) photocatalysis. Environmental life cycle assessments indicate that while natural solar photolysis can have a large footprint, the addition of iron and hydrogen peroxide improves process efficiency and sustainability. Utilizing renewable energy can reduce the environmental footprint of these oxidation processes by up to 87.5% (p. 67-68).

### Photocatalysis Mechanisms and Parameters
The heterogeneous photocatalysis process relies on the formation of reactive hydroxyl radicals ($^\bullet OH$) that oxidize pollutants. Key process factors include:
* Catalyst Mass: Increasing the catalyst concentration enhances degradation rates until light scattering and particle agglomeration occur (p. 69).
* pH Influence: pH affects the catalyst's surface charge and the availability of oxidizing species. Specifically, the reaction mechanism shifts as shown by the following surface equilibria:
$(M - OH)_{surface} + H^+ \leftrightarrow (M - OH_2^+)_{surface}$ (p. 69)
$(M - OH)_{surface} + OH^- \leftrightarrow (M - O^-)_{surface} + H_2O$ (p. 69)
* Inhibitors: Anions like chloride, nitrate, and sulfate typically inhibit performance by competing for adsorption sites and scavenging radicals (p. 69).

### Semiconductor and Material Developments
Because TiO2 has a large bandgap (3.0–3.2 eV), it primarily requires UV light, which constitutes only a small fraction of solar radiation (p. 68, 70). Research aims to enhance visible-light sensitivity through chemical doping, photosensitization, and the deposition of noble metals (Au, Ag) or the use of alternative semiconductors like BiOCl (p. 70). Notably, BiOCl microspheres have demonstrated superior efficiency in removing carbamazepine (CBZ) due to their unique interlayer structure and ability to efficiently separate electron-hole pairs (p. 70-71).

### Pharmaceutical and Dye Degradation
Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are essential for persistent pollutants like carbamazepine and textile dyes, which resist conventional biological treatments (p. 71, 72). The study finds that mixtures of pollutants (such as different dyes) can sometimes exhibit synergistic oxidation effects, enhancing the overall mineralization degree compared to treating single-component solutions (p. 71). Future progress depends on stabilizing catalysts on inert supports to prevent efficiency loss due to suspended solids and maximizing solar energy utilization (p. 70).

This chapter explores the synthesis of active GPS Radio Occultation (RO) data with passive infrared and microwave satellite soundings to improve global atmospheric water vapor and temperature profiling.

### The Role and Limitations of Remote Sensing
Water vapor is a critical greenhouse gas and a key component of the Earth's hydrological and energy balance. While infrared (IR) and microwave (MW) sensors, such as AIRS and ATMS, have provided long-term global monitoring, they face inherent limitations:
- IR sounders provide high-quality data but are restricted to clear-sky conditions and struggle with lower tropospheric moisture measurements.
- MW sounders operate in all-weather conditions but suffer from uncertainties in moisture and hydrometeor retrievals, particularly in the lower troposphere (p. 104).

### Advantages of GPS Radio Occultation (RO)
The GPS RO technique provides a complementary active sensing capability with the following advantages:
- It functions in all-weather conditions regardless of clouds or precipitation.
- It offers high vertical resolution, ranging from 100 m near the surface to 1.5 km at 40 km altitude.
- It lacks satellite-to-satellite bias and instrument drift, making it exceptionally stable for climate monitoring (p. 104, 106).

### Physical Basis for Integration
The RO signal measures atmospheric phase delays, which are used to derive bending angles and refractivity. Refractivity ($N$) is mathematically related to pressure ($P$), temperature ($T$), and the partial pressure of water vapor ($PW$) by the formula:
$N = 77.6 \frac{P}{T} + 3.73 \times 10^5 \frac{PW}{T^2}$
Because RO measurements are highly sensitive to water vapor in the moisture-rich troposphere, they provide essential vertical structural information that passive sensors lack (p. 105, 106, 108).

### Combined Inversion Systems
Researchers have developed enhanced inversion algorithms, such as RO-MiRS (for ATMS) and RO-AIRS, which use a one-dimensional variational (1D-var) approach. By combining the unique sensitivities of active RO data with the broad spatial coverage of passive IR/MW radiometers:
- Retrieval accuracy in the lower troposphere is improved.
- Global water vapor vertical profiles and Total Precipitable Water (TPW) estimates are more consistent and better validated against independent ground-based GPS data (p. 108, 109).

Overall, the synthesis of these distinct sensor types creates a more comprehensive and accurate dataset for numerical weather prediction, climate change assessment, and atmospheric chemistry studies (p. 103, 109).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Based on the description of Green Chemistry, which statement best characterizes its role beyond being a technical scientific discipline?
options: It is solely a set of strict industrial regulations.
It functions as a philosophy that influences how science and engineering are approached.
It is a niche methodology restricted to academic laboratory settings.
It is a reactive approach focused primarily on waste disposal legislation.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly describes Green Chemistry as not just a science, but a philosophy and nearly a religion, highlighting its role in radicalizing the business of doing science and engineering.

questions.question: What is implied by the text's mention of the American Chemical Society Green Chemistry & Engineering Conferences?
options: They are exclusively designed for policy makers and business owners, not scientists.
They serve as a forum to disseminate the ideals and possibilities of the Green Chemistry approach.
They focus on the history of traditional chemistry rather than modern applications.
They are mandatory events required to practice chemistry legally.
questions.explanation: The text notes that attending these conferences instills the ideals of green chemistry, suggesting they serve as a platform for promoting the philosophy and its potential.

questions.question: Which of the following topics is explicitly listed as a 'Key Element' of Green Chemistry in the provided content?
options: Economic profit maximization
Life-Cycle Analysis
Traditional solvent usage
Regulatory enforcement strategies
questions.explanation: Life-Cycle Analysis is directly listed under the section '1: Key Elements of Green Chemistry' as one of the constituent points.

questions.question: How does the author describe the intent of Green Chemistry regarding the 'business of doing science'?
options: To keep the business of science exactly as it has been traditionally practiced.
To radicalize how science and engineering are conducted.
To minimize the importance of engineering in scientific processes.
To replace science entirely with religious philosophy.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions that Green Chemistry represents a novel approach to 'radicalizing the business of doing science and engineering'.

questions.question: Which category listed in the content specifically targets the materials used to facilitate chemical transformations?
options: Alternative Reagents
Reaction Types
Hazards
Back Matter
questions.explanation: Alternative Reagents refers to the substances used in chemical reactions, which is a key element in designing more sustainable chemical processes.

cards.front: Beyond being a science, how is Green Chemistry often described?
cards.back: It is described as a philosophy and nearly a religion.

cards.front: Which professional organization hosts the Green Chemistry & Engineering Conferences?
What is the primary goal of applying Green Chemistry to science and engineering?
cards.back: To radicalize and transform the business and practice of doing science and engineering.

cards.front: Name one of the key elements of Green Chemistry mentioned in the lesson.
cards.back: Life-Cycle Analysis, Hazards, Alternative Solvents, Alternative Reagents, or Reaction Types/Design.

cards.front: What does 'Life-Cycle Analysis' refer to in the context of Green Chemistry?
cards.back: The assessment of the environmental impact of a product throughout its entire existence.

cards.front: Why is 'Hazards' listed as a key element of Green Chemistry?
cards.back: Because Green Chemistry aims to minimize or eliminate the inherent hazards of chemical substances and processes.

cards.front: What role do 'Alternative Solvents' play in Green Chemistry?
cards.back: They replace traditional, often toxic or hazardous, solvents with safer, more environmentally friendly options.

cards.front: How do 'Alternative Reagents' contribute to the principles of Green Chemistry?
cards.back: They are utilized to perform chemical transformations with less waste, higher efficiency, and lower toxicity.

cards.front: What is the focus of 'Reaction Types, Design, and Efficiency' in Green Chemistry?
cards.back: Optimizing chemical reactions to be more atom-economical, energy-efficient, and generate less waste.

cards.front: What is the purpose of the 12 Principles (implied by the key elements) of Green Chemistry?
cards.back: To provide a framework for scientists and engineers to design products and processes that minimize environmental and health risks.

slides.title: Green Chemistry Applications
bullets: Overview of diverse green chemistry applications.
Focus on sustainable synthesis and environmental remediation.
slides.notes: This lesson introduces key applications of green chemistry in material science, medicine, energy, and environmental monitoring. It highlights the transition toward sustainable industrial practices.

slides.title: Nanocarbon-Polymer Composites
bullets: Sustainable synthesis of nanocarbon materials (p. 15).
Tailoring nanocarbons with polymers for benign materials.
Applications in energy storage and environmental remediation.
Advances in surface functionalization and stability (p. 16).
slides.notes: Nanocarbon-polymer composites are emerging as functional materials that combine durability with environmental sustainability for industrial and ecological challenges.

slides.title: Green Synthesis of Nanocomplexes
bullets: Green synthesis of La(III) nanomaterial complexes (p. 33).
Characterization via SEM and TEM for morphology.
Anticancer activity testing against Caco2 and MCF-7 cells.
Fabrication of medicated nanofibers for wound dressing (p. 34).
slides.notes: This research demonstrates how green chemistry can produce metal-based complexes with significant therapeutic potential, specifically for cancer treatment and advanced medical wound management.

slides.title: Infrared Irradiation as Green Energy
bullets: Sustainable activation of organic chemical reactions (p. 49).
Efficient extraction method for natural secondary metabolites.
Categorization into NIR, MIR, and FIR regions (p. 50).
Use of natural catalysts to enhance reaction efficiency.
slides.notes: Infrared irradiation provides a cleaner, more efficient energy source for synthetic chemistry, aligning with UNESCO sustainable development goals and fossil-fuel reduction targets.

slides.title: PEG-Mediated Click Chemistry
bullets: Regioselective synthesis of 1,2,3-triazoles (p. 71).
Polyethylene glycol-400 as green reaction media.
High atom economy and reduced environmental impact.
One-pot synthesis avoids handling toxic organic azides.
slides.notes: Utilizing PEG-400 as a recyclable solvent enables a more benign 'click chemistry' process, specifically for creating pharmaceutical and agrochemical scaffolds efficiently.

slides.title: Green Water Treatment Strategies
bullets: Photodecomposition of pharmaceuticals using solar energy.
Comparison of photo-Fenton and TiO2 photocatalysis (p. 81).
Role of metallic nanoparticles in visible-light activation.
Reducing environmental footprints in water purification (p. 82).
slides.notes: Advanced oxidation processes leveraging solar energy provide a sustainable path to removing persistent pollutants like antibiotics and endocrine disruptors from wastewater.
